steps.title: Identify what counts as “before Harlem”
steps.content:
The student's goal is clear: emphasize accomplishments before moving to Harlem (1914).

The notes identify exactly two works from the pre-Harlem period — Songs of Jamaica (1912) and Constab Ballads (1912), both published while McKay lived in Jamaica.

Any choice that emphasizes these two works meets the goal; any choice that mixes in post-Harlem works like Harlem Shadows (1922) or Home to Harlem (1928) fails to emphasize the pre-Harlem period.

steps.title: Match the goal to the choices
steps.content:
Choice B directly accomplishes the goal: it names the two pre-Harlem works and explicitly positions them as early accomplishments despite McKay's greater fame as a Harlem Renaissance writer. The contrast structure ("Although he is best known...") ensures the reader understands these 1912 publications in Jamaica are noteworthy in their own right.

Choice A lists all four works without temporal distinction, muddling the emphasis. Choice C shifts focus entirely to the 1914 move and McKay's Harlem-era reputation. Choice D contradicts itself — it claims to discuss the pre-Harlem period but then cites post-Harlem works like Harlem Shadows (1922) and Home to Harlem (1928), with titles that explicitly invoke Harlem.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
